examples.title: File Open and Read System Call Flow
examples.description: Pseudocode showing what happens when a process opens and reads a file
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the layered approach in file system operations. The open() call traverses directories to locate the file's inode, while read() uses the inode's block pointers to locate and retrieve data, potentially using the buffer cache.
examples.timeComplexity: O(log n) for open (directory traversal), O(1) for read if cached
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for each system call, plus buffer cache space

examples.title: Indexed File Allocation - Block Lookup
examples.description: Algorithm to locate the k-th block of a file using indexed allocation
examples.explanation: The algorithm shows how multi-level indexing allows files larger than what direct pointers can address. The trade-off is that accessing a block far into a large file requires multiple disk reads (one for each level of indirection).
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for direct, O(2) for single indirect, O(3) for double indirect, O(4) for triple indirect (disk reads)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - only stores indices during lookup

examples.title: Free Space Management - Bitmap Operations
examples.description: Implementation of free block allocation and deallocation using a bitmap
examples.explanation: This demonstrates bitmap-based free space management. The allocation algorithm scans for consecutive free blocks to improve locality. Modern file systems use more sophisticated algorithms to balance speed, memory usage, and fragmentation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) worst case for allocation where n is number of blocks, O(1) for freeing specific blocks
